content.assessment: Networked retention has some coalition-building logic in its favor—it can absorb existing informal coalitions (church-operator takeovers, the rare Erdőkürt-style social-capital case) into a more formalized structure—but its central political risk is that it tries to institutionalize what has so far only occurred through scarce, idiosyncratic local social capital, and it depends on voluntary inter-municipal cooperation in a context where informal pupil-competition dynamics already work against cooperation.
opportunities: Formalizing networked clusters could substitute institutional design for the scarce social capital currently required for ad hoc local rescues, broadening the set of villages that could plausibly retain schools
strengths: Church takeover as an alternative-operator coalition has already scaled substantially (60% growth 2001-2011, further 47% 2011-2014), showing an existing, growing coalition mechanism that networked retention could formalize or complement
threats: Documented informal inter-municipal competition over pupil enrollment could undermine the voluntary cooperation the clustering model requires, since municipalities have incentives to protect their own pupil counts rather than share them
weaknesses: The one documented Hungarian success case (implied by the evidence) depended on rare bonding/bridging social capital, and there is no demonstrated mechanism yet for reproducing this reliably through formal networks
